story:
On Thomas’s first day of school, I held his hand, and he held mine wrong—his fingers limp, like a dead spider in my palm. He stared at the enamel-white steeple and whispered, “This is your place. Why am I going here?” Routine anchored him; change knocked him off course. Still, if I told him to follow, he would, hopping behind me like a nervous bird.

The other children mocked him—how he talked, how he moved, how his skin shone with a thin, greasy sheen. He never seemed to understand their jokes. When they shut him out of football, he simply watched, his head jerking after the ball as if each motion surprised him. Later, when they’d gone, I found him stroking the ball, eyes bright, as if it were alive. He always did things like that. Once, I caught him whispering to one of Dad’s grandfather clocks, head bent to its heartbeat. “Stop it, Tom. It’s not alive,” I said. “How do you know?” he asked. Another time he knocked down a photo of Mum, then cried over the fractured frame. Mum called Dad in, pleased to see him sad for her picture. But I knew he was mourning the frame. Later, I saw him kissing the splintered wood, trying to make it better.

Our house brimmed with photos, as if Mum could stack memories against the walls. Six of me, one for each year I’d been there. Only seven of Thomas, though he was eight and not adopted. On the mantel: baby Thomas in Mum’s glowing arms beside a magnificent clock. Then a gap, and Thomas at three—Mum’s smile stiffer, her body angled away, her arm not quite touching him. The clock had vanished from the house. A family friend once mentioned Mum being in the hospital while Dad practically lived at the shop that year. When I asked more, he went pale.

Sometimes I think they adopted me so Thomas would have an older brother. I was nine—old enough to keep him safe. Animals wouldn’t have him: cats hissed, dogs startled as if they heard a frequency the rest of us missed. He loved them anyway, chasing cats with a shared chocolate bar in hand. I hate that I understood their recoil. His skin felt almost plastic; I had to stop myself from flinching when he hugged me. Mum tried to hide her revulsion and sometimes softened, close to the glowing mother in the first photo. Dad watched Thomas the way he watched his clocks—warm, intent, proud. He never let me into his workshop. “Too complicated, Edward. More… in touch with life.” Mum said coldly, “We don’t want that life for you.” But Dad took Thomas there. Thomas came back buzzing, renewed. Hurt, I avoided his hug and let him chase a cat alone.

He returned an hour later with blood in his hair. In the bathroom, the red was veined with black, slick as oil. My fingers found two tiny screws. A square of his scalp dropped open.

Gears. Great golden wheels, and smaller ones beneath them, pulleys and levers and springs, each ticking in its own time. My stomach flipped. Thomas blinked at me. “Will I have to go back with Dad now?”

The puzzle locked into place: the workshop, the missing clock, Mum in hospital, the gap in the photos, Dad’s fevered work. I closed the panel and tightened the screws. “Come on,” I said. “Dad will know what to do.” Thomas peered into me, searching for a pattern.

“Edward,” he said quietly. “I love you.” He gripped my hand, uncertain, pressing now and then to check I was still there.

“I love you too,” I said. I pressed back, feeling his plastic skin and the small, steady tick beneath my thumb, and held him close—in this world he was built for rather than born into.
